overview.title: Constitution of the United States of America
overview.subtitle: A modern replacement draft built around democratic legitimacy, anti-authoritarian safeguards, and readable constitutional architecture.
overview.top_strength: The architecture is now materially cleaner and the simulator still shows a closure-oriented system with 0 unresolved obligations even under a larger, harsher suite
overview.top_weakness: The remaining weakness is no longer missing machinery; it is a mix of deadline-driven political nonperformance, a few judicial and institutional delay chains, and residual code-like density in a handful of articles

docs.title: Preamble
docs.summary: We the People of the United States, all of us without exception, establish this Constitution.

docs.title: Article I — The Electoral System
docs.summary: Establishes the right to vote, the mechanics of all federal elections, the rules for political party participation, and the citizens' initiative process. Election administration is delegated to the Electoral Commission under Article XII.

headings.text: Section 4 — Elections to the House of Representatives
Section 5 — Elections to the Regional Assembly
Section 9 — Party Qualification and Representation Threshold
Section 13 — Recall of Elected Federal Officials

docs.title: Article II — The Legislature
docs.summary: Establishes the structure, composition, powers, and procedures of the federal legislature. The legislature consists of two chambers: the House of Representatives and the Regional Assembly. Together they form the Congress of the United States.

headings.text: Section 7 — Exclusive Powers of the House
Section 8 — Exclusive Powers of the Regional Assembly
Section 15A — Mandatory Scheduling for Constitutionally Required Votes
16.2 — Subpoena Power and Expedited Judicial Enforcement
16.4 — Perjury and False Statements Before Congress
16.6 — Executive Privilege: Definition, Scope, and Limits
16.7 — Adverse Inference for Bad-Faith Privilege Claims

docs.title: Article III — The Executive
docs.summary: Establishes the structure, powers, and limits of the executive branch. The President is both head of state and head of government. Executive power is derived from this Constitution and may not be exercised beyond its limits regardless of congressional authorization.

headings.text: Section 4 — The Cabinet and Executive Departments
Section 8 — Commander in Chief and War Powers
Section 14 — Public Recall of the President
15.1 — The Core Principle: No Immunity for Criminal Conduct
15.3 — Absolute Prohibitions: No Immunity in Any Circumstance
15.4 — Heightened Procedural Protection for Other Official-Capacity Conduct
15.6 — Former Presidents: No Special Post-Presidential Immunity

docs.title: Article IV — The Judiciary
docs.summary: Establishes the structure, composition, independence, and jurisdiction of the federal judiciary. Judicial power is vested in courts established by this Constitution and by Congress. The judiciary is the final arbiter of the meaning of this Constitution.

headings.text: Section 3 — Appointment of Supreme Court Justices
Section 5 — Federal Courts Below the Supreme Court

docs.title: Article V — Rights and Freedoms
docs.summary: Enumerates and protects the civil and political rights of all persons within the United States. This Article replaces and expands the Bill of Rights and all rights-bearing amendments of the prior Constitution. Rights enumerated here are a floor — states may expand but may not reduce them. The enumeration of specific rights shall not be construed to deny or disparage others retained by the people.

headings.text: Section 5 — Security of Person and Home
Section 6 — Due Process and Fair Treatment
Section 8 — The Right to Keep and Bear Arms
Section 9 — Rights of the Accused and Punishments

docs.title: Article VI — Democratic Integrity
docs.summary: Protects the democratic system itself from subversion, foreign interference, and capture of the information environment. This Article has no equivalent in the prior Constitution — it treats bad-faith actors as a design constraint rather than an anomaly. It establishes the rules governing electoral subversion, foreign meddling in elections and politics, and media plurality. Enforcement is primarily through the Constitutional Organs established in Article XII.

headings.text: Section 1 — Anti-Subversion and Protection of Democratic Processes
Section 2 — Campaign Finance and Political Money
Section 3 — Corruption, Conflicts of Interest, and Self-Dealing
5.4 — Prohibition on acting on foreign electoral assistance
5.5 — Shell entities and covert foreign political influence
Section 7 — Media Pluralism and Information Integrity

docs.title: Article VII — Campaign Finance and Political Money
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework governing money in federal and state elections. The core principles are that voters have a right to know who is funding political power, that political office must remain realistically accessible without wealth or large private donors, and that legislatures may regulate political money to prevent corruption, domination, concealment, and foreign influence.

headings.text: Section 3 — Spending Limits and Outside Spending
Section 5 — Transition, Inaugural, and Post-Election Money

docs.title: Article VIII — Government Ethics
docs.summary: Establishes the ethics rules that govern how federal officials behave in office. These provisions address corruption, financial conflicts of interest, civil service merit protection, lobbying, and the integrity of financial markets. Enforcement is primarily through the Accountability Commission (Article XII). Democratic integrity provisions — anti-subversion, foreign interference in elections, and media pluralism — are in Article VI. General constitutional rules governing the structure, classification, and independence of federal agencies are in Article XIII; this Article states the substantive ethics and market-integrity requirements that those agencies and officials must obey.

headings.text: Section 1 — Corruption, Conflicts of Interest, and Self-Dealing
1.8 — Permitted investments: the index fund rule
1.13 — Beneficial ownership disclosure for federal contractors
1.14 — Prohibition on use of government resources for campaign purposes
2.2 — Independence of the financial market regulator
2.5 — No settlement without admission for major fraud

docs.title: Article IX — Citizenship and National Membership
docs.summary: Establishes equal citizenship, national membership, domicile principles, overseas political participation, and constitutional rules for citizenship status. Citizenship is a legal status under this Constitution, but it is also a guarantee against hereditary caste, arbitrary exclusion, and unequal membership in the political community.

headings.text: Section 5 — Loss, Renunciation, and Conflict of Membership

docs.title: Article X — Federalism and the States
docs.summary: Establishes the division of authority between the federal government and the states. The United States is a federal republic in which the national government and state governments each possess defined sovereign authority. Neither is a mere creation of the other. This Article replaces the prior Constitution's Articles IV and VI and the Tenth Amendment.

headings.text: Section 2 — Powers of the Federal Government
Section 3 — Powers Reserved to the States
Section 6 — Washington D.C. and the National Capital
Section 7 — Territories and Admission of New States

docs.title: Article XI — Amendments
docs.summary: Establishes the processes by which this Constitution may be amended, the provisions that may never be amended, and the judicial mechanisms that enforce both.

docs.title: Article XII — Constitutional Organs
docs.summary: Establishes two independent Constitutional Organs: the Electoral Commission and the Accountability Commission. These are not branches of government. They do not make law, set policy, or govern. They enforce the rules and integrity of the system that the three branches operate within.

docs.title: Article XIII — Federal Agencies and the Administrative State
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework governing federal agencies, commissions, and administrative bodies created by Congress. The administrative state is the primary instrument through which the federal government implements law in practice. Its structure, independence, accountability, and integrity are matters of constitutional importance. This Article does not enumerate specific agencies — those are created, modified, and abolished by statute — but establishes the structural rules that all agencies must follow and the protections that certain categories of agency must receive. Where another Article imposes agency-specific substantive duties or additional safeguards, those provisions control in addition to this Article.

headings.text: Section 2 — Division of Authority Over Agencies
Section 4 — Protections for Independent Regulatory Agencies
Section 5 — Protection Against Abolition and Restructuring

docs.title: Article XIV — Taxation and Public Revenue
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional principles governing taxation and public revenue. This Article concerns how public money is raised. Article XV governs how money is borrowed, appropriated, and spent.

docs.title: Article XV — Budget, Public Credit, and Appropriations
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional principles governing borrowing, appropriations, fiscal continuity, budget integrity, classified spending, and public credit. Article XIV governs how revenue is raised. This Article governs how public money is authorized, financed, disclosed, and spent.

headings.text: Section 3 — Appropriations and Continuity of Government
Section 7 — Long-Term Responsibility and Emergency Finance

docs.title: Article XVI — War Powers and National Security
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework for the use of military force and the conduct of war. The war-making power belongs to Congress. The President directs authorized operations and responds to immediate threats. This Article governs armed conflict, combat operations, and other uses of military force.

headings.text: Section 1 — Commander in Chief and War Powers Authority
Section 15 — Technology and Supply Chain Security

docs.title: Article XVII — Foreign Policy and National Security
docs.summary: Establishes the constitutional framework for economic security, foreign influence defense, critical infrastructure protection, foreign intelligence collection, gray-zone response authority, alliance obligations, and technology security. These provisions address modern threats that fall below or alongside conventional military conflict, including economic coercion, information warfare, cyber attacks on civilian systems, and supply-chain vulnerabilities. Article XVI remains the exclusive framework for war, sustained hostilities, and other uses of military force.

headings.text: Section 7 — Technology and Supply Chain Security

docs.title: Article XVIII — Social, Economic, and Affirmative Rights
docs.summary: Establishes social, economic, environmental, and affirmative rights of all persons within the United States. These rights impose positive duties on government to maintain constitutional floors of protection against destitution, exclusion, and preventable harm inconsistent with a minimally dignified life. They complement the civil and political rights in Article V. The general principles of Article V Section 1, including the rights floor, the prohibition on permanent suspension, absolute rights, and the rule that enumeration does not deny unenumerated rights, apply equally to the rights in this Article. Judicial enforcement under this Article is limited to deciding whether government has met the constitutional floor, whether government has impermissibly backslid from that floor, and what timely lawful remedy is required. Courts may not govern program design in the place of the political branches.

docs.title: Article XIX — Ratification and Transition
docs.summary: Governs ratification, legal continuity, supersession of the prior constitutional order, first-cycle implementation, and transition to the institutions established by this Constitution. This Article is read to favor prompt, lawful, and democratically legitimate establishment of the new constitutional order.

headings.text: Section 3 — Carried-Forward and Superseded Constitutional Provisions

docs.title: Constitution Index
docs.summary: This is the master index for the revised Constitution. Each major component lives in its own file for easier editing and review. This document tracks the status of each part and provides navigation.

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.summary: This document describes the revised draft in broad terms: what kind of constitution it is, what problems it is trying to solve, and how it compares to the current United States Constitution and to common constitutional models in other democracies.

docs.summary: This document records conclusions drawn from the constitutional flow simulator and its generated reports. Each entry should cite the relevant scenario outputs so recurring bottlenecks can be tracked over time.
